title: Security: 13 known CVEs in examples/celery/requirements.txt
description:
## Summary

The `examples/celery/requirements.txt` file contains pinned dependency versions with **13 known security vulnerabilities** (1 HIGH, 11 MEDIUM, 1 LOW), identified via Trivy filesystem scan.

## Affected File

`examples/celery/requirements.txt` — pip-compiled lockfile for the Celery example application.

## Vulnerabilities

### HIGH (1)

| Package | CVE | Installed | Fixed | Description |
|---------|-----|-----------|-------|-------------|
| werkzeug | [CVE-2024-34069](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-34069) | 2.3.3 | 3.0.3 | Code execution on developer's machine via debugger |

### MEDIUM (11)

| Package | CVE | Installed | Fixed | Description |
|---------|-----|-----------|-------|-------------|
| jinja2 | [CVE-2024-22195](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-22195) | 3.1.2 | 3.1.3 | HTML attribute injection via xmlattr |
| jinja2 | [CVE-2024-34064](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-34064) | 3.1.2 | 3.1.4 | Non-attribute character acceptance in keys |
| jinja2 | [CVE-2024-56201](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-56201) | 3.1.2 | 3.1.5 | Sandbox breakout through malicious filenames |
| jinja2 | [CVE-2024-56326](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-56326) | 3.1.2 | 3.1.5 | Sandbox breakout through indirect reference to format method |
| jinja2 | [CVE-2025-27516](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2025-27516) | 3.1.2 | 3.1.6 | Sandbox breakout through attr filter selecting format method |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2023-46136](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2023-46136) | 2.3.3 | 2.3.8 | High resource consumption (DoS) |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2024-49766](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-49766) | 2.3.3 | 3.0.6 | safe_join not safe on Windows |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2024-49767](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2024-49767) | 2.3.3 | 3.0.6 | Resource exhaustion when parsing file data in forms |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2025-66221](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2025-66221) | 2.3.3 | 3.1.4 | DoS via Windows device names in path segments |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2026-21860](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2026-21860) | 2.3.3 | 3.1.5 | safe_join() allows Windows special device names with compound extensions |
| werkzeug | [CVE-2026-27199](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2026-27199) | 2.3.3 | 3.1.6 | safe_join() allows Windows special device names |

### LOW (1)

| Package | CVE | Installed | Fixed | Description |
|---------|-----|-----------|-------|-------------|
| flask | [CVE-2026-27205](https://avd.aquasec.com/nvd/cve-2026-27205) | 2.3.2 | 3.1.3 | Information disclosure via improper caching of session data |

## Previous Attempts

- PR #5987 attempted to fix only CVE-2024-34069 (werkzeug) but was closed without merge (2026-04-09). This issue covers all 13 CVEs comprehensively.

## Proposed Fix

Update the pinned versions in `examples/celery/requirements.txt`:
- `flask` 2.3.2 → 3.1.3
- `werkzeug` 2.3.3 → 3.1.6
- `jinja2` 3.1.2 → 3.1.6
- `blinker` 1.6.2 → 1.9.0 (flask 3.x dependency)
- `itsdangerous` 2.1.2 → 2.2.0 (flask 3.x dependency)
- `markupsafe` 2.1.2 →

title: Add a built-in is_safe_redirect() helper to handle browser URL normalization (backslash → forward slash)
description:
Modern browsers normalize backslashes in navigation URLs (e.g., `\\evil.com`) into forward slashes (`//evil.com`). This creates a discrepancy between how Flask/Werkzeug evaluates redirect targets and how user agents ultimately interpret them.

A common pattern used by Flask developers to prevent protocol-relative redirects is:

```python
if not url.startswith("//"):
    return redirect(url)
```

However, the following payload bypasses this logic:

```
\\evil.com
```

Flask/Werkzeug evaluates this as a safe relative path (`startswith("//") == False`), while browsers normalize it to:

```
https://evil.com
```

and perform an external redirect. This normalization behavior is consistent across Chrome, Firefox, and Safari.

As Flask does not provide a built-in redirect validation helper, developers routinely implement ad-hoc string checks that do not account for browser normalization rules, resulting in an ecosystem-wide class of open-redirect flaws.

**Feature Request**

Introduce an official, browser-aware redirect safety utility in Flask or Werkzeug, similar to Django’s `is_safe_url`, to standardize redirect validation across the framework. The helper should:

1. Normalize backslashes to forward slashes before validation.
2. Reject protocol-relative URLs (`//example.com`).
3. Optionally enforce allowed host checks.
4. Provide a documented, recommended pattern for safe redirects.

**Rationale**

- This issue is not a flaw in Flask’s redirect implementation but a recurring security footgun for developers due to inconsistent handling between application logic and browser URL normalization.
- Providing an official helper reduces the likelihood of open-redirect vulnerabilities in applications built on Flask.
- This is framework hardening rather than a security bug report, and it aligns with the expectations set by other frameworks that offer standardized redirect-safety utilities.

This feature would give developers a reliable and recommended approach to validating redirect targets securely, reducing reliance on fragile custom logic.

title: [Security] Default session HMAC digest should be SHA-256, not SHA-1
description:
## Summary

Flask's `SecureCookieSessionInterface` uses SHA-1 as the default HMAC digest for signing session cookies. SHA-1 has been deprecated by NIST (since 2011) and is no longer recommended for cryptographic use.

## Location

`src/flask/sessions.py`, line 293:

```python
digest_method = staticmethod(_lazy_sha1)

title: Update: HTTP_Headers_Cheat_Sheet
description:
## What is missing or needs to be updated?
Serving user-provided files, such as attachments, without special headers can lead to various security vulnerabilities, including Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) and Content Security Policy (CSP) bypass. This is because browsers execute these files by default. Currently, the cheat sheet only covers the `X-Content-Type-Options` header and partially the `Content-Type` header.


## How should this be resolved?
1. Include the `Content-Disposition` header with a recommendation to use `Content-Disposition: attachment` for serving user-provided files. According to the [RFC](https://httpwg.org/specs/rfc6266.html#rfc.section.4.2), `attachment` should prompt the user to save the response locally, rather than process it normally. Without the header, a browser may default to [`inline` mode](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/Content-Disposition#as_a_response_header_for_the_main_body), which typically processes the content.
2. Suggest using `Content-Type: application/octet-stream` for serving user-provided files. This may prevent a browser from executing a file. According to [MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Guides/MIME_types#applicationoctet-stream):
> application/octet-stream
> This is the default for binary files. As it means unknown binary file, browsers usually don't execute it, or even ask if it should be executed. They treat it as if the [Content-Disposition](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Reference/Headers/Content-Disposition) header was set to attachment, and propose a "Save As" dialog.
> ...
> Most web servers send unrecognized resources as the application/octet-stream MIME type. For security reasons, most browsers do not allow setting a custom default action (like "Open in Word") for such resources, forcing the user to save it to disk to use it.

Using these headers with `X-Content-Type-Options` should prevent a browser from processing user-provided files.

title: Update: NPM_Security_Cheat_Sheet - Expand slopsquatting section
description:
## What is missing or needs to be updated?

The current cheat sheet mentions slopsquatting briefly in section 10 but does not provide sufficient guidance. Given that AI coding assistants are now widely used by developers, slopsquatting is an increasingly relevant attack vector that deserves more detailed coverage.

Specifically missing:
- How slopsquatting differs from typosquatting
- Why AI tools are particularly vulnerable to hallucinating package names
- Concrete steps developers can take to verify AI-suggested packages
- How to configure npm to reduce risk from unverified packages

## How should this be resolved?

Expand the existing slopsquatting subsection in section 10 with more detailed guidance and practical prevention steps for developers using AI coding assistants.

title: Update: gRPC_Security_Cheat_Sheet
description:
<!--- Please complete the sections below -->

## What is missing or needs to be updated?
The current cheat sheet covers message size limits for gRPC streams but does not address limiting the number of messages or the total duration of a streaming session.  
Without such limits, clients can flood the server or keep streams open indefinitely, potentially causing resource exhaustion.


## How should this be resolved?
Add a bullet under Input Validation and Data Security → Implement Message Size Limits:
- Limit streaming sessions and message counts to prevent resource exhaustion. Monitor and enforce maximum messages per stream and maximum session duration.

Optionally, we can include a minimal Go example:

```go
// Go - Secure streaming with message & duration limits
func (s *server) StreamData(req *pb.StreamRequest, stream pb.MyService_StreamDataServer) error {
    const maxMessages = 1000          // Max messages per stream
    const maxDuration = 2 * time.Minute // Max total session time

    start := time.Now()
    msgCount := 0

    for {
        if time.Since(start) > maxDuration {
            return status.Error(codes.ResourceExhausted, "stream duration exceeded")
        }

        if msgCount >= maxMessages {
            return status.Error(codes.ResourceExhausted, "maximum messages exceeded")
        }

        msg, err := stream.Recv()
        if err == io.EOF {
            break
        }
        if err != nil {
            return err
        }

        // Process message safely
        processMessage(msg)

        msgCount++
    }

    return nil
} 
```

title: New CS proposal: Subdomain Takeover Prevention
description:
## What is the proposed Cheat Sheet about?

Subdomain takeover prevention — specifically, how developers and DevOps teams can avoid leaving dangling DNS records that allow attackers to claim ownership of their subdomains.

I do pentesting and bug bounty work regularly, and this is honestly one of the most common findings I run into. A company spins up a service on Heroku or S3 or Azure, creates a CNAME for it, then months later the service gets deprovisioned but nobody remembers to clean up the DNS record. An attacker finds it, claims the resource, and now they're serving content on your subdomain. It's embarrassingly easy to exploit and surprisingly hard for organizations to keep track of.

## What security issues are commonly encountered related to this area?

The core issue is dangling DNS records — CNAMEs (and sometimes A records) pointing to cloud resources that no longer exist. But it branches out into several specific scenarios:

- **Expired cloud resources**: S3 buckets that got deleted, Azure web apps that were removed, GitHub Pages repos that were made private or deleted, Heroku apps that were decommissioned. Each cloud provider has slightly different takeover mechanics.
- **Forgotten proof-of-concept subdomains**: Dev and staging environments that get set up for a sprint and never torn down.
- **Third-party SaaS deprovisioning**: Company stops using a service like Fastly, Shopify, or Zendesk but leaves the DNS pointing there.
- **Impact is worse than people think**: Once someone takes over your subdomain, they can serve phishing pages on your domain, steal cookies scoped to the parent domain, bypass CSP policies that trust *.yourdomain.com, and in some cases compromise OAuth flows that whitelist the subdomain.

On HackerOne alone there are hundreds of subdomain takeover reports filed every year. It's consistently one of the most reported vulnerability classes in bug bounty programs.

## What is the objective of the Cheat Sheet?

Give engineering teams a practical, actionable guide to prevent subdomain takeovers. Not just "audit your DNS" but actually useful stuff:

- Clear explanation of what subdomain takeover is and the real-world impact (cookie theft, phishing on your domain, CSP bypass, etc.)
- Cloud-specific guidance — what makes AWS S3, Azure, GitHub Pages, Heroku, Fastly, Netlify, etc. vulnerable to takeover, and how to configure each one safely
- DNS hygiene practices: how to set up processes to catch dangling records before attackers do
- Detection and monitoring approaches (both DIY and tooling-based)
- Prevention checklist for DevOps teams — things like "delete DNS records BEFORE deprovisioning the cloud resource, not after"
- How to handle the organizational/process side, because this is fundamentally a problem of teams not communicating during infrastructure changes

I'd be happy to write the initial PR if this proposal gets accepted. I have plenty of real-world notes from testing this stuff.

title: New CS proposal: Enterprise Application Security Program Governance
description:
<!--- Please complete the sections below -->

## What is the proposed Cheat Sheet about?
This cheat sheet is about how to build and run an application security program at the enterprise level. Most OWASP resources are written for developers dealing with specific vulnerabilities whereas this one is targeted for the people responsible for the bigger picture like AppSec managers, security leads, and anyone tasked with making application security work consistently across organizations.
It covers the practical side of program governance, how to structure your team, how to prioritize and track vulnerabilities at scale, how to integrate security into the development lifecycle without slowing teams down, and how to measure and report on program effectiveness in a way that actually means something to leadership.
I had reviewed the existing cheat sheet library and there is currently nothing that addresses application security at the program governance level. The closest one's touch on individual practices like threat modeling or dependency management, but none of them look at how to run the program itself. That gap is what this cheat sheet is trying to fill.

## What security issues are commonly encountered related to this area?
The security issues in this space are less about individual vulnerabilities and more about the organizational and structural failures that allow vulnerabilities to persist at scale. Some of the most common ones I have seen are listed below:
No clear ownership: Security findings get raised but nobody is clearly accountable for fixing them. Teams point fingers at each other and issues stay open indefinitely.
Risk prioritization done wrong: Organizations either treat every vulnerability as critical and burn out their developers, or they rely solely on CVSS scores which have no context about the actual business impact of a finding.
Security bolted on at the end: Development teams finish building something and security is only involved at the last minute, making it expensive and disruptive to fix anything meaningful.
No visibility into the attack surface: Large organizations often do not have a clear picture of all the applications they own, which means unknown and unmanaged risk sitting in forgotten or legacy systems.
Metrics that do not mean anything: Programs report on number of scans run or tools deployed rather than actual risk reduction, making it impossible to tell whether the program is actually working.
Security champions in name only: Organizations stand up security champion programs but give champions no time, training, or authority to actually drive change in their teams.
Compliance mistaken for security: Teams focus on passing audits rather than fixing real risk, creating a false sense of security that does not hold up when something goes wrong.

title: Update: Authentication Cheat Sheet – API-based authentication flow reference
description:
## What is missing or needs to be updated?

The Authentication Cheat Sheet provides detailed guidance on individual mechanisms (sessions, reauthentication, MFA, token handling), but it does not clearly describe *where* authentication and authorization decisions should occur in a modern API-based architecture.

For developers building API-first systems (e.g., REST APIs, FastAPI/Django APIs, microservices), it is often unclear which responsibilities belong to:
- login endpoints,
- middleware,
- token refresh endpoints,
- or mid-session risk escalation logic.

This lack of architectural context can lead to inconsistent or insecure implementations, even when individual recommendations are followed.

## How should this be resolved?

Add a short, diagram-style documentation subsection to the Authentication Cheat Sheet titled:

**“Where authentication decisions should occur in an API-based system”**

The subsection would:
- Describe the request lifecycle in an API-driven architecture
- Explain the responsibilities of:
  - Login endpoints
  - Authentication/authorization middleware
  - Token refresh endpoints
  - Mid-session reauthentication or risk escalation
- Be framework-agnostic and text-based (no images required)
- Reference existing sections such as Session Management and Reauthentication After Risk Events

This addition would connect existing guidance into a practical architectural model without changing any existing recommendations.
author: Alvi24-hub

title: [UPDATE] Modernize Python Secrets Retrieval in Serverless Cheat Sheet using AWS Lambda Extension
description:
### Description
The current Python examples in the `Serverless_Security_Cheat_Sheet.md` for secrets management utilize the `boto3` SDK to fetch secrets directly. While functional, this is no longer the recommended best practice for high-performance or cost-efficient serverless applications.

### Proposed Improvement
I propose updating the "Secrets Management" section to demonstrate the use of the **AWS Parameters and Secrets Lambda Extension**. 

**Why this is better:**
1. **Security:** It uses a local HTTP endpoint (`localhost:2773`), minimizing the footprint of the AWS SDK within the function and reducing the risk of logging sensitive credential retrieval attempts.
2. **Performance:** The extension provides a managed, in-memory cache. This significantly reduces latency (cold starts) and prevents unnecessary API calls to Secrets Manager on every invocation.
3. **Cost:** It drastically reduces the number of API requests, which are billed at $0.05 per 10,000 calls.

### Changes
I will replace the existing `boto3` example with a `urllib3` implementation that interacts with the extension's local cache. I will also add a brief technical note regarding the requirement of adding the extension as a Layer.

title: Update: CSRF Cheat Sheet
description:
<!--- Please complete the sections below -->

## What is missing or needs to be updated?
<!--- Provide a description of the problem with the current Cheat Sheet -->

The docs discourage the "Naive Double-Submit Cookie Pattern" because it is vulnerable to "certain attacks", linking to the ["Bypassing CSRF Protections" paper](https://owasp.org/www-chapter-london/assets/slides/David_Johansson-Double_Defeat_of_Double-Submit_Cookie.pdf).

That paper describes a class of attacks where an attacker is able to set cookies on the target site, either through a subdomain or a MITM attack. They set the CSRF cookie, then send a request with a token matching that cookie. The server verifies the attacker controlled cookie against the token in the attacker's request, and since they match the CSRF protection is bypassed.

Instead the docs recommend the "Signed Double-Submit Cookie" pattern, where the token is signed with a secret key. But signing alone does nothing to prevent this attack! All it does is add an extra step, where the attacker must first get a valid token by making a request. They then set that token in the cookie. Without signing, they could have set any random value in the cookie and it would have worked.

From what I can tell, signing only helps when it is bound to the session. Then the attacker can't inject a valid CSRF cookie without also injecting a valid session cookie, and they can't do that while keeping the user's existing session. But the docs don't state this as a requirement. They recommend that you bind it to the user's session to "further enhance security", making it sound like signing alone is sufficient.

Given that binding to the session is what actually makes this more secure, it isn't clear to me why signing is necessary at all. It seems like a hash over the session ID and some random bytes would serve the same purpose, without requiring the user to manage secret keys.

There are a lot of other things in the docs that are pretty confusing:

> This ensures that an attacker cannot create and inject their own, known, CSRF token into the victim's authenticated session

As stated above, signing does nothing to prevent an attacker who can set cookies from injecting their own CSRF token.

> The system's tokens should be secured by hashing or encrypting them.

I don't think encryption would be safe here unless it was authenticated encryption.

"The system's tokens" is a bit confusing too, because there are no tokens to hash or encrypt; the token is the mac (or hash) of the session ID.

> The server-side session ID (e.g. PHP or ASP.NET). This value should never leave the server or be in plain text in the CSRF Token.

Don't session IDs necessarily leave the server in the cookie on every request? I think they are trying to say it shouldn't be in the non HTTP-Only cookie?

> Generate a random value (preferably cryptographically random) to ensure that consecutive calls within the same second do not produce the sa

title: Update: LDAP_Injection_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet
description:
## What is missing or needs to be updated?
<!--- Provide a description of the problem with the current Cheat Sheet -->

After a casual reading, the Cheat Sheet seems to suggests that even passwords should always be escaped. When this is taken at face value, it can cause all kinds of issues, mainly stuff not working as expected any more (see https://github.com/dexidp/dex/issues/3433).

The `Safe Java Escaping Example` wrongly assumes that a password check against LDAP is done via LDAP filter and escapes the password, when actually password checking against an ldap server is (or at least should be) exclusively done via BIND request which safely accepts the password in unfiltered plaintext. 

## How should this be resolved?
<!--- What changes should be made to the Cheat Sheet to address this? -->
The usual methods for password authentication against LDAP should be mentioned:
* Single-Step Auth:
  - BIND-Request with DN constructed from template like `uid=${USERID},ou=people,dc=example,dc=com`. Whatever is inserted in that template should be escaped as needed. Password from user input is given to BIND call as is.
* Three-Step-Auth:
  - BIND with credentials limited to searching - these would be configured in the app.
  - `SEARCH` LDAP for users DN like `(&(uid=${USERID})(deactivated=0))` or something. Again, anything inserted into that query should be escaped as needed
  - BIND with DN from search result above, if any. Password from user input is given to BIND call as is.
 
These two both assume the LDAP server to require (non-anonymous) BIND before allowing access to user data, which I think is (or should be) default or at least best practice. So `Enabling Bind Authentication` should be moved up in the document's focus, i.e. "To prevent LDAP Injection, first the LDAP server needs to be configured properly...". Even if it's out of scope of this document, proper safety precautions should be mentioned - "Simple BIND transmits plain text passwords to the server, so it should only be used with an encrypted connection" or "If you **really** need to use unencrypted connection, make sure to only use SASL BIND with challenge protocols etc. to prevent password sniffing."

The `Safe Java Escaping Example` should be updated to reflect either one of the above methods and no longer use the password in a search filter.

title: New CS proposal: Incident Response for Web Applications
description:
<!--- Please complete the sections below -->

## What is the proposed Cheat Sheet about?
The proposed cheat sheet, titled "Incident Response for Web Applications," aims to provide a practical guide for handling security incidents related to web applications. It will cover the essential steps, communication plans, evidence preservation, and analysis required during an incident response scenario.

## What security issues are commonly encountered related to this area?
Common security issues related to incident response for web applications that will be addressed in this cheat sheet include:

1. **Common Web Application Threats:**
   - Identification and response strategies for common threats, including SQL injection, cross-site scripting, and data breaches.

2. **Denial of Service Attacks:**
   - Guidelines for detecting and mitigating distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks targeting web applications.

3. **Unauthorized Access and Account Compromise:**
   - Procedures for responding to incidents involving unauthorized access, compromised accounts, and user data breaches.

4. **Data Integrity and Tampering:**
   - Strategies for detecting and responding to incidents related to data integrity and unauthorized tampering of web application content.

5. **Malicious File Uploads:**
   - Response plans for incidents involving malicious file uploads and potential exploitation of file upload vulnerabilities.

## What is the objective of the Cheat Sheet?
The objective of the "Incident Response for Web Applications" cheat sheet is to equip web developers, security teams, and incident responders with practical guidance for effectively managing and mitigating security incidents. The cheat sheet aims to:

- Provide a structured incident response plan tailored to web application security incidents.
- Offer communication templates for informing stakeholders and users during security incidents.
- Outline evidence preservation techniques for forensic analysis and legal purposes.
- Enhance the ability to quickly identify and respond to security incidents in a web application environment.

## What other resources exist in this area?
- **OWASP Incident Response Guide:**
  - Refer to the [OWASP Incident Response Guide](https://owasp.org/www-project-incident-response/) for additional insights into incident response best practices.

- **NIST Cybersecurity Framework:**
  - Explore the [NIST Cybersecurity Framework](https://www.nist.gov/cyberframework) for a comprehensive framework that includes incident response guidelines.

- **Incident Response Blogs and Articles:**
  - Review blogs and articles from incident response experts and organizations for real-world incident response scenarios and lessons learned.

## References
- [OWASP Incident Response Guide](https://owasp.org/www-project-incident-response/)
- [NIST Cybersecurity Framework](https://www.nist.gov/cyberframework)

title: Axios standardizes headers to be case-insensitive, which causes issues for case-sensitive APIs
description:
### Describe the bug

Hi,

I have a question regarding the capitalization of headers in Axios requests. By default, it seems that Axios capitalizes header names (e.g., "content-type" becomes "Content-Type" in some versions).

This behavior can cause issues when working with case-sensitive APIs, such as AWS API Gateway, which require headers to follow specific casing ([as noted in the AWS documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway/latest/developerguide/api-gateway-known-issues.html#:~:text=Header%20names%20and%20query%20parameters,back%20by%20your%20integration%20endpoint)).

I understand that this decision may follow [RFC 2616](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec4.html#sec4.2), which states that HTTP header names are case-insensitive. However, this approach can make Axios challenging to use in environments where case-sensitive headers are required.

I’ve noticed other issues opened on this topic, but they have been closed without a final solution, with the decision being to follow the specification:

Issue https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/4509
Issue https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/413

If there are other related issues, I couldn’t find them here.

Is there any way to configure Axios to send headers in a case-sensitive manner, or is there a workaround to address this issue? Any insights or suggestions would be greatly appreciated!

### To Reproduce

I encountered the error while using AWS pre-signed URLs. The backend of my system generates this upload URL, and the frontend sends the file to the obtained endpoint. My API is written in .NET 8.

### Code snippet
This is an example of a manual PUT request without Axios, where headers are set to be case-sensitive:

```js

PUT /programa_29151_capitulo_9.fdx?AWSAccessKeyId=*****&Expires=1730412840&x-amz-security-token=*****&Signature=***** HTTP/1.1
accept: application/json
content-type: 
```

In comparison, here’s the same PUT request using Axios:

```js
PUT /programa_11111_capitulo_99.fdx?AWSAccessKeyId=*****&Expires=1730412968&x-amz-security-token=*****&Signature=***** HTTP/1.1
Accept: application/json
Content-Type: 
```

The only difference between these two requests is the capitalization of the content-type and accept headers, with the manual request having these headers in lowercase (content-type and accept), while Axios automatically capitalizes them (Content-Type and Accept). This is a real example I’ve encountered; the first request succeeds, but the second returns a 403 Forbidden error. I’ve hidden the other request details as they are exactly the same.

### Axios Version

1.7.7

### Node.js Version

22.2.0

### Additional Library Versions

```bash
React 18.2.0
```

Thank you!

title: Prototype pollution vulnerability in lodash assign / assignIn wrapper flow
description:
Hi, we are a security team. We found a prototype pollution vulnerability in your project.

The issue affects the wrapper-based `assign` / `assignIn` flow in `lodash` when the target object is `Object.prototype`.

In this code path, properties from user-controlled input are copied into the target object through dynamic property assignment. Since special keys are not filtered, an attacker may be able to pollute the global prototype.

## Impact

If an attacker can control the source object and the wrapped target is `Object.prototype`, this may lead to global prototype pollution and affect other objects in the same runtime.

## Proof of concept

### Case 1: `assign`

```js
const _ = require('lodash');

const wrapped = _(Object.prototype);
wrapped.assign({ polluted: true }).value();

console.log(({}).polluted); // true
```

### Case 2: `assignIn`

```js
const _ = require('lodash');

const wrapped = _(Object.prototype);
wrapped.assignIn({ polluted: true }).value();

console.log(({}).polluted); // true
```

## Details

We confirmed the following cases:

- `assign`
  - sink: `./_baseClone.js:61`

- `assignIn`
  - sink: `./_baseClone.js:61`

The root cause is that attacker-controlled properties are written into the target object without blocking special prototype-related keys.
author: Vincebye

title: Prototype pollution vulnerability in lodash.mapkeys
description:
Hi, we are a security team. We found a prototype pollution vulnerability in your project.

The issue affects `lodash.mapkeys`.

The vulnerable behavior happens when the iteratee result is used as a property name in a dynamic assignment. If the iteratee returns a special key such as `__proto__`, or if input data leads to that key being used during assignment, prototype pollution may occur.

## Impact

An attacker may be able to modify the prototype of the result object, which can cause prototype pollution in the affected runtime.

## Proof of concept

### Case 1: iteratee returns `__proto__`

```js
const mapKeys = require('lodash.mapkeys');

const result = mapKeys({ anyKey: { polluted: true } }, () => '__proto__');

console.log(result.polluted); // true
```

### Case 2: input contains `__proto__`

```js
const mapKeys = require('lodash.mapkeys');

const result = mapKeys({ ['__proto__']: { polluted: true } }, (value, key) => key);

console.log(result.polluted); // true
```

## Details

We confirmed the following cases:

- sink: `./index.js:2222`
- sink: `./index.js:420`

The root cause is that untrusted key material can flow into dynamic property writes without blocking special prototype-related keys.
author: Vincebye

title: Prototype pollution vulnerability in lodash.mapvalues
description:
Hi, we are a security team. We found a prototype pollution vulnerability in your project.

The issue affects `lodash.mapvalues`.

The vulnerable behavior happens when user-controlled object keys are reused in dynamic property assignment while building the result object. If a special key such as `__proto__` is present in the input, prototype pollution may occur.

## Impact

An attacker may be able to modify the prototype of the produced result object, which can cause prototype pollution in the affected runtime.

## Proof of concept

### Case 1: parsed input contains `__proto__`

```js
const mapValues = require('lodash.mapvalues');

const result = mapValues(
  JSON.parse('{"__proto__":{"polluted":true}}'),
  (value) => value
);

console.log(result.polluted); // true
```

### Case 2: direct input with special key

```js
const mapValues = require('lodash.mapvalues');

const result = mapValues(
  { ['__proto__']: { polluted: true } },
  (value) => value
);

console.log(result.polluted); // true
```

## Details

We confirmed the following cases:

- sink: `./index.js:2229`
- sink: `./index.js:420`

The root cause is that untrusted keys can flow into dynamic property writes without blocking special prototype-related keys.

title: lodash.template npm package still vulnerable to CVE-2021-23337 (unfixed since 2021)
description:
## Summary

The standalone npm package [`lodash.template@4.5.0`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/lodash.template) is still vulnerable to **CVE-2021-23337** (Command Injection via the `variable` option). This was fixed in the main `lodash` package in version **4.17.21**, but the modularized standalone package `lodash.template` was **never republished** with the fix.

## Impact

- **CVE**: [CVE-2021-23337](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-35jh-r3h4-6jhm)
- **Severity**: High (CVSS 7.2)
- **Affected package**: `lodash.template@4.5.0` (latest on npm, published 2018)
- **Status**: The fix exists in `lodash.js` ([line 180, 14969](https://github.com/lodash/lodash/blob/main/lodash.js#L180)) but was never propagated to the standalone package

## The Fix (already in lodash.js)

```js
var reForbiddenIdentifierChars = /[()=,{}\[\]\/\s]/;

// In the template function:
else if (reForbiddenIdentifierChars.test(variable)) {
  throw new Error(INVALID_TEMPL_VAR_ERROR_TEXT);
}
```

This validation prevents injection of arbitrary code through the `variable` option in `_.template()`.

## Current Situation

- `lodash@4.17.21+` → **Fixed** ✅
- `lodash.template@4.5.0` → **Still vulnerable** ❌ (last published 2018)
- GitHub/Dependabot continues to flag this in projects that depend on `lodash.template` transitively (e.g., via `generator-jhipster`)
- The package is marked as deprecated on npm, but no patched version was ever released

## Request

Could the `lodash.template` standalone package be **regenerated from the current lodash.js source and republished to npm** (e.g., as `4.5.1`) to include the CVE-2021-23337 fix?

This would resolve Dependabot alerts for thousands of projects that transitively depend on `lodash.template`.

## Workaround

As a workaround, I've created a patched fork with the same fix applied:
https://github.com/khayashi4337/lodash.template-fixed

This can be used in `package.json` resolutions:
```json
"resolutions": {
  "lodash.template": "https://github.com/khayashi4337/lodash.template-fixed.git"
}
```

title: Time sensitive: GitHub Actions Cache rate limits (from GitHub staff)
description:
To preserve the health of our services and reliably serve all GitHub Actions Cache users, the following rate limit will take effect immediately: 

[200 cache entry uploads per minute per repository](https://github.blog/changelog/2026-01-16-rate-limiting-for-actions-cache-entries/).

If you exceed this limit, you will receive a 429 response and an error message indicating that you have exceeded the rate limit. 

You should not retry your request until after the time specified in the `Retry-After` header has passed. If your request continues to fail due to this rate limit, implement exponential backoff between retries, and throw an error after a specific number of retries.

This limit helps prevent abuse and denial-of-service attacks and ensures that the service remains available for all users. 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Introduce the needed changes to your library so that the rate limit errors are retried as per the recommended schedule. 

### Update your actions and libs

If you are using any of the following actions or depend on any of these libraries:
- https://github.com/mozilla-actions/sccache-action
- https://github.com/mozilla/sccache
- https://github.com/apache/opendal
- https://github.com/DeterminateSystems/magic-nix-cache

Introduce the needed changes to maintain a high cache hit/miss ratio, or follow-up with the authors of those libraries on the progress of these changes.

### Thrashing

It has also come to our attention that a substantial number of repositories adopting this library (or action) are exhibiting consistent cache thrashing behavior. 

Cache thrashing occurs when cache entries are frequently evicted and re-created in a continuous cycle, preventing effective cache utilization. This happens when your workflow generates more cache data than can be stored within your available cache storage quota. 

By default, GitHub Actions provides *10 GB of cache storage per repository*. When this limit is reached, GitHub automatically evicts the least recently used cache entries to make room for new ones. If your workflows consistently produce cache data that exceeds this limit, the cache is constantly being written and evicted—resulting in thrashing. 

This leads to: 

- Wasted compute time spent creating caches that are immediately evicted 
- Increased network bandwidth consumption 
- Little to no performance benefit from caching 

To avoid thrashing, consider increasing your cache storage limit. For more information, see ["Usage limits and eviction policy."](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/dependency-caching#usage-limits-and-eviction-policy)

title: [FR] Disable eval() usage by default
description:
### What is the problem this feature will solve?

`eval() `and `Function() `in Node.js allow executing arbitrary code at runtime. While powerful, they are a common vector for security vulnerabilities and accidental code injection. Currently, `eval()` is enabled by default, which means that even minor mistakes or untrusted dependencies can execute code dynamically, increasing security risks.

By disabling `eval()` by default, Node.js can provide a safer runtime out of the box. This change encourages developers to use safer alternatives such as vm or strict sandboxing, reducing potential attack surfaces in Node.js applications.

As you can see in this [commit](https://github.com/midudev/preguntas-entrevista-react/commit/72c3e5a2684108db9af3fccbc4d09b2356c74e0d#diff-0a9670300d2139e7a1a7e37ace2536b0d0c890c7681dcaa12d3e410d72547f8e), you can somehow insert arbitrary code on a commit and not even notice.

This [video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ikdCG4goKIE) (in spanish, by @midudev), explains how they attacked him through this method. Is crazy

### What is the feature you are proposing to solve the problem?

Introduce a new opt-in flag --enable-eval in Node.js.
- Default behavior: eval() and Function() are disabled in the runtime. Any attempt to use them without the flag will throw an error.
- Opt-in: Users can explicitly enable eval() for legacy applications or specific use cases by passing `node --enable-eval index.js`
- Benefits:

- - Improves default security for Node.js applications.
- - Prevents accidental dynamic code execution in production.
- - Allows developers to consciously decide when they need eval().

author: devbrewster

title: Disallow custom authentication class to return user = `None`
description:
Hello there!
Currently it's allowed that an custom authentication class returns a tuple containing `(None, None)`. This causes the authentication to be considered successful and sets `request.user = None` and this can cause errors that are hard to track where they came from.
I know that this wrongly implemented by the `CustomAuthentication` class, the user should've raised an `AuthenticationFailed` exception instead. But this allows the user to shoot itself on the foot.

Example of a bad implemented authentication class:
```python
from rest_framework.authentication import BaseAuthentication

class MyDumbAuthentication(BaseAuthentication):
  def authenticate(self, request):
    return None, None
```

Later if you have a permission check for example, you would see the following error:
```python
from rest_framework.permissions import BasePermission

class MyPermCheck(BasePermission):
  def has_permission(self, request, view):
    return request.user.has_perm("foo.bar")

# raises AttributeError("'NoneType' object has no attribute 'has_perm'")
```

It would be nice if rest framework disallowed this totally wrong implementation.

## Checklist

- [x] Raised initially as discussion
- [x] This is not a feature request suitable for implementation outside this project. Please elaborate what it is:
  - [ ] compatibility fix for new Django/Python version ...
  - [x] other type of bug fix
  - [ ] other type of improvement that does not touch existing code or change existing behavior (e.g. wrapper for new Django field)
- [x] I have reduced the issue to the simplest possible case.

title: SQLMap fails to dump LONGTEXT/XML column via UNION (truncated output / SQL syntax errors)
description:
### Description

When dumping a column containing large XML/JSON data, sqlmap detects a working UNION injection but fails to dump the column correctly. The tool reports truncated output or SQL syntax errors.

### Environment

* sqlmap version: (e.g. 1.8.x / latest from GitHub)
* Python version: (e.g. Python 3.12)
* OS: Windows 10
* DBMS: MySQL
* Injection type detected: UNION query (NULL) – 20 columns

### Command used

```
sqlmap -u "TARGET_URL" \
--technique=U \
--union-cols=20 \
--threads=5 \
-D DATABASE -T TABLE -C COLUMN \
--dump
```

### Observed behavior

sqlmap detects UNION injection successfully:

```
Type: UNION query
Title: MySQL UNION query (NULL) - 20 columns
```

However when dumping the column containing large XML/JSON data, sqlmap returns errors such as:

```
possible server trimmed output detected (probably due to its length and/or content)
```

and sometimes:

```
Uncaught mysqli_sql_exception: You have an error in your SQL syntax near '[{"xml": "<?xml version="1.0"...'
```

### Workaround

Using boolean-based extraction works:

```
--technique=B
```

But the extraction becomes extremely slow.

### Expected behavior

Since UNION-based injection is confirmed working, sqlmap should ideally handle long column values (XML/JSON) without truncation or syntax errors, or automatically switch to a safer extraction method.

### Additional information

The column contains serialized API responses beginning with:

```
[{"xml": "<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
```

which may exceed the HTTP response length or break SQL syntax during UNION extraction.

title: [ERROR] invalid character detected. retrying.. infinite loop / very slow progress on stable time-based blind (MySQL/MariaDB) with frequent 500 errors
description:
### Environment / Setup

- sqlmap version: 1.7#stable (also tested on latest dev branch 1.10.2.18#dev - same behavior)
- OS: Windows 11 (also tested on Linux / git master)
- DBMS: MySQL >= 5.0.0 (MariaDB fork)
- Web stack: Laravel + Apache
- Injection type: time-based blind (Parameter replace - substraction)
  Confirmed payload example: od=(SELECT 7020 FROM (SELECT(SLEEP(10)))UDQx)
- Technique: --technique=T (or BUSETQ - T dominant)
- Tamper scripts: between,space2comment,apostrophemask (tried reducing to only space2comment - same)
- Charset / encoding: --hex --encoding=utf-8 (tried utf8, ISO-8859-2, removed)
- Other flags: --level=5 --risk=3 --prefix="'" --suffix="'" --time-sec=10..12 --delay=3..4 --retries=5..6 --threads=1 --no-cast (when compatible)

### Problem description


sqlmap.py -u "https://www.asdfasdfasdf.pl/asdfasdfy/asdfasdfasdf-asdfasdfasd/asdfasdfasdfasd?ciag=&filtruj=1&bezcyfr=1&bezmyslnikow=1&idn=1&od=test&do=&funk=1&regio=1&dotpl=1&wygasaza=1" -p "od" --dbms=mysql --level=3 --risk=3 --random-agent --batch --tamper=between,space2comment,apostrophemask --time-sec=12 --delay=4 --timeout=90 --retries=6 --threads=1 --hex --encoding=utf-8 --cookie="laravel_session=asdfasdfasdfasdf465a4sd65f4a65s4dg65asdg" --headers="Host: www.asdgasdgasdg.pl\nAccept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,image/webp,image/apng,*/*;q=0.8\nAccept-Encoding: gzip, deflate\nAccept-Language: pl-PL,pl;q=0.9,en-US;q=0.8,en;q=0.7\nReferer: https://www.asdfgasdgasdg.pl/asdfasd/asdfasdf/asdfasdf" --technique=BUSETQ --prefix="'" --suffix="'" -D asdasd -T lara_users --columns

When dumping columns or data from a table (e.g. lara_users which has 76 columns), sqlmap finds characters correctly at first, but then enters an **infinite / very long retry loop** with:
[ERROR] invalid character detected. retrying..
[WARNING] increasing time delay to X seconds
textDelay value gradually increases (2s → 3s → 4s → ... up to hundreds of seconds), but validation never succeeds.

This makes enumeration extremely slow or practically stuck (e.g. one column name can take 30–60+ minutes or never finish).

### Observed behavior / logs snippet

title: TLS SNI does not match custom Host header when targeting IP
description:
**Describe the bug** When targeting an HTTPS endpoint using its direct IP address in the URL (`-u https:///`) and specifying a custom Host header via `--headers="Host: "` or `--host=""`, sqlmap does not set the TLS Server Name Indication (SNI) extension to match the custom Host header. This results in a `421 Misdirected Request` error from servers that require the TLS SNI to match the HTTP Host header. I'm targeting the direct IP to avoid CDN, but the web server needs a host header to resolve the correct application. **To Reproduce** 1. Run a command targeting an IP but forcing a domain Host header: `python sqlmap.py -u https://127.0.0.1/users?name=abbas --headers="Host: tryhackme.com" -v 6` or `python sqlmap.py -u https://127.0.0.1/users?name=abbas --host="tryhackme.com" -v 6` 2. Observe the traffic output. The TLS handshake occurs with the IP address (or blank) as the SNI, while the HTTP request sends `Host: tryhackme.com`. 3. See error: `421 Misdirected Request`. **Expected behavior** Sqlmap should either automatically use the value of a user-supplied `Host` header for the TLS SNI extension during the handshake, or provide an explicit command-line flag (e.g., `--sni="tryhackme.com"`) to allow users to set the SNI manually, similar to modern curl implementations or Postman. **Screenshots** N/A - Console output provided below. **Running environment:** - sqlmap version: 1.10.2.14#dev - Installation method: git clone - Operating system: Ubuntu 22.04 - Python version: 3.13.3 **Target details:** - DBMS: Unknown (connection blocked before reaching the application) - SQLi techniques found by sqlmap: N/A - WAF/IPS: Cloudflare - Relevant console output: ```log [05:15:27] [INFO] testing 'Microsoft SQL Server/Sybase stacked queries (comment)' [05:15:27] [PAYLOAD] AX');WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'-- [05:15:27] [TRAFFIC OUT] HTTP request [#37]: GET /users?name=AX%27%29%3BWAITFOR%20DELAY%20%270%3A0%3A5%27-- HTTP/1.1 Host: tryhackme.com Authorization: Basic User-Agent: sqlmap/1.10.2.14#dev (https://sqlmap.org) Accept: */* Accept-Encoding: gzip,deflate Connection: close [05:15:27] [TRAFFIC IN] HTTP response [#37] (421 Misdirected Request): Date: Wed, 11 Mar 2026 04:15:26 GMT Server: Apache Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains Content-Length: 362 Connection: close Content-Type: text/html; charset=iso-8859-1 URI: https://127.0.0.1/users?name=AX%27%29%3BWAITFOR%20DELAY%20%270%3A0%3A5%27-- 421 Misdirected Request

Misdirected Request

The client needs a new connection for this request as the requested host name does not match the Server Name Indication (SNI) in use for this connection.

[05:15:27] [DEBUG] got HTTP error code: 421 ('Misdirected Request') ```
author: abbasudo

title: ZAP does not shutdown in command line mode if AJAX Spider fails to start the browser
description:
### Describe the bug

The process hangs (does not finish) after the automation plan succeeded if the job `spiderAjax` is included into the plan.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior

1. Create file `config.yaml` with the following content:
    ~~~
    --- # OWASP ZAP automation configuration file, for more details see https://www.zaproxy.org/docs/automate/automation-framework/
    env:                                   # The environment, mandatory
      contexts :                           # List of 1 or more contexts, mandatory
        - name: my_context                 # Name to be used to refer to this context in other jobs, mandatory
          urls:                            # A mandatory list of top level urls, everything under each url will be included
            - "http://example.com"
    jobs:
      - type: spiderAjax
    ~~~
2. Run ZAP and see the output:
    ~~~
    $ zap.sh -cmd -autorun "$(pwd)/config.yaml"
    Found Java version 17.0.2
    Available memory: 25343 MB
    Using JVM args: -Xmx6335m
    Job spiderAjax started
    Job spiderAjax found 0 URLs
    Job spiderAjax finished
    Automation plan succeeded!
    ~~~
3. Note that the process has not finished after the automation plan succeeded.

### Expected behavior

The process has finished after the automation plan succeeded.

### Software versions

~~~

title: InformationDisclosureSuspiciousCommentsScanRule should provide the comment leading up to `from` instead of just the word `from`
description:
### Describe the bug:

https://github.com/zaproxy/zap-extensions/blob/2e441bfb3d068fed7fd790f33b23e1b80aae6823/addOns/pscanrules/src/main/java/org/zaproxy/zap/extension/pscanrules/InformationDisclosureSuspiciousCommentsScanRule.java#L68-L85
includes `FROM`

https://github.com/zaproxy/zap-extensions/blob/2e441bfb3d068fed7fd790f33b23e1b80aae6823/addOns/pscanrules/src/main/java/org/zaproxy/zap/extension/pscanrules/InformationDisclosureSuspiciousCommentsScanRule.java#L93
looks for `//` followed by any number of characters

And then it looks for the combination of the `//`, **any** number of characters, and any of the items including `from`
https://github.com/zaproxy/zap-extensions/blob/2e441bfb3d068fed7fd790f33b23e1b80aae6823/addOns/pscanrules/src/main/java/org/zaproxy/zap/extension/pscanrules/InformationDisclosureSuspiciousCommentsScanRule.java#L101-L105

and it does things case-insensitively:
https://github.com/zaproxy/zap-extensions/blob/2e441bfb3d068fed7fd790f33b23e1b80aae6823/addOns/pscanrules/src/main/java/org/zaproxy/zap/extension/pscanrules/InformationDisclosureSuspiciousCommentsScanRule.java#L247

For a minified file, like monaco, that combination isn't particularly exciting or surprising.

There are 30 hits for `//.*\bfrom\b` in https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/monaco-editor@0.55.1/+esm

Instead of using the not terribly helpful rule, a slightly fancier rule of:
```
//(?:.(?!//)){1,100}?\bfrom\b
```
Would yield 3, which are closer to valid (although really not remotely close, but still closer):
```js
\n\t// Multiple vert.position by 2,-2 to get it into clipspace which ranged from -1 to 1\n\tvsOut.position = vec4f(\n\t\t// Make everything relative to top left instead of center\n\t\tvec2f(-1, 1) +\n\t\t((vert.position * vec2f(2, -2)) / layoutInfo.canvasDims) * glyph.size +\n\t\t((cell.position * vec2f(2, -2)) / layoutInfo.canvasDims) +\n\t\t((glyph.origin * vec2f(2, -2)) / layoutInfo.canvasDims) +\n\t\t(((layoutInfo.viewportOffset - scrollOffset.offset * vec2(1, -1)) * 2) / layoutInfo.canvasDims),\n\t\t0.0,\n\t\t1.0\n\t);\n\n\tvsOut.layerIndex = cell.textureIndex;\n\t// Textures are flipped from natural direction on the y-axis, so flip it back\n\tvsOut.texcoord = vert.position;\n\tvsOut.texcoord = 
```

```js
createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg","path");path.setAttribute("d",`M ${0} ${line.from} 
```

Also, instead of providing evidence `from`, the code should report the capture. Given the constraint that a capture should have no more than 100 characters between the `//` and the word, that wouldn't be a terribly painful amount of content to show a reviewer, and would enable them to make a reasoned decision about the notice.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. Run a scan on a system that includes https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/monaco-editor@0.55.1/+esm with InformationDisclosureSuspiciousCommentsScanRule

### Expected behavior:

1. Provide more context than just `from` for evidence
2. Use a smaller window for characters after `//` (100

title: Out of database space error with 2.17.0
description:
### Describe the bug:

**Description**
Some active scans that ran fine with 2.16.1 now fail halfway the scan on the following error:

> Out of database space, ZAP will effectively be unusable.
> Shutting down ZAP due to space issues...

**Version used**
The problem occurs using version 2.17.0.

**Additional context**
Disk space error handling was recently changed with #9148; maybe that introduced the issue? At least, the error message _Out of database space, ZAP will effectively be unusable._ was added as part of that PR.

Up until 2.16.1, the same scans completed successfully. In 2.17.0 no more. After reverting to 2.16.1, the scans are successful once more.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior:

This happens consistently for several of my scenarios, when ZAP is well in to the scanning process / has been scanning for more than an hour. 
These scenarios involve _active scans_ for urls recorded by the ZAP proxy for fixed paths through the application, started via the _/ascan/action/scan/_ endpoint.

This only happens for *some* fixed paths through the application. Perhaps only for those where the urls exceed some threshold.

### Expected behavior:

Scanning should complete without database error, like in earlier versions.
Maybe errors occurred with earlier versions as well, but were just not visible. But at least they did not fail the scan.

title: Duplicate HTML id attributes in FormsForValueGenerator.html cause DOM and accessibility issues
description:
### Describe the bug:

The file zap/src/main/resources/org/zaproxy/zap/spider/parser/htmlform/FormsForValueGenerator.html contains multiple HTML elements with duplicate id attribute values (e.g., id="id1" and id="id2" reused within the same document).
According to the HTML specification, id values must be unique within a document. Duplicate IDs can lead to incorrect DOM behavior, broken JavaScript interactions, and accessibility issues.
This issue was identified via static analysis (SonarCloud rule Web:S7930) and confirmed by manual inspection.
This is a standards-compliance and reliability bug, not a security vulnerability.

### Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. Open the file:
zap/src/main/resources/org/zaproxy/zap/spider/parser/htmlform/FormsForValueGenerator.html
2. Inspect the HTML source.
3. Observe multiple elements sharing the same id values (e.g., id="id1" and id="id2").
4. (Optional) Run a static analysis tool (e.g., SonarCloud or HTML validator) to confirm duplicate ID warnings.

### Expected behavior:

Each HTML element should have a unique id attribute within the document, in compliance with the HTML specification.

This ensures:
Correct DOM access via JavaScript
Predictable CSS targeting
Proper association of <label> elements
Correct behavior of assistive technologies (screen readers)

### Software Versions:

ZAP: latest main branch (observed directly in repository source)
OS: Not applicable (static resource)

### Screenshots:

_No response_

### Errors from the zap.log file:

Not applicable — this issue does not produce runtime errors and was identified through static analysis and code inspection.

### Additional context:

SonarCloud flags this as a Critical reliability issue (Web:S7930).
This is not a security issue and does not introduce an exploitable condition.
Fixing this would improve HTML correctness, accessibility (WCAG compliance), and maintainability.
I am happy to submit a pull request to fix the duplicate IDs if this approach is acceptable.

title: False Positive -
description:
The following details are reported by Zap for our solution:

CWE 200 - Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

An information exposure is the intentional or unintentional disclosure of information to an actor that is not explicitly authorized to have access to that information.

The information either

is regarded as sensitive within the product's own functionality, such as a private message; or
provides information about the product or its environment that could be useful in an attack but is normally not available to the attacker, such as the installation path of a product that is remotely accessible.

Description:
This method is now most commonly used in REST services, PATCH is used for **modify** capabilities. The PATCH request only needs to contain the changes to the resource, not the complete resource.

Solution:
Implement measures to obfuscate or disguise information about the system's platform, web application software technology, backend database version, configurations, and network architecture/topology. This can include:

1. **Platform and Software Diversity:** Use a mix of technologies and platforms to make it harder for attackers to build an accurate profile.

2. **False Information:** Introduce fake or misleading information in system responses to confuse fingerprinting tools.

3. **Response Randomization:** Randomize certain elements in responses to make it difficult for attackers to consistently identify the system.

4. **Firewall Rules:** Implement firewall rules to block or limit the effectiveness of fingerprinting techniques.

5. **Regular Updates:** Keep software, platforms, and configurations up-to-date to patch known vulnerabilities and prevent accurate identification based on outdated information.

There is no one-size-fits-all solution, and a combination of these measures may be most effective.

---

This has been flagged as a false positive based on the following:  

The relative path for a link identified by ZAP as a finding is from an error page returned by the server due an invalid request URL (See the raw zap report xml since SRM does not display the actual path). The URL cannot be exploited since it is an error page.

title: Third Party Dependency Vulnerabilities
description:
Hello,

I was doing some security vulnerability scanning of Zaproxy  2.16.1 and noticed that some third party jars have some reported CVEs associated with them. Mainly to do with `org.bouncycastle:bcprov-jdk18on` and `commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils `

I was curious if you have plans to update these dependencies in the future and what timeline that might have. 

Also if you know that Zaproxy is not effected by these CVEs that would also be helpful.

Thank you

Name | Vulnerable package | Severity | Description | Fixed in
-- | -- | -- | -- | --
CVE-2024-29857 | org.bouncycastle:bcprov-jdk18on | HIGH | An issue was discovered in ECCurve.java and ECCurve.cs in Bouncy Castle Java (BC Java) before 1.78, BC Java LTS before 2.73.6, BC-FJA before 1.0.2.5, and BC C# .Net before 2.3.1. Importing an EC certificate with crafted F2m parameters can lead to excessive CPU consumption during the evaluation of the curve parameters. | 1.78
CVE-2024-30172 | org.bouncycastle:bcprov-jdk18on | HIGH | An issue was discovered in Bouncy Castle Java Cryptography APIs before 1.78. An Ed25519 verification code infinite loop can occur via a crafted signature and public key. | 1.78
CVE-2025-48734 | commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils, commons-beanutils:commons-beanutils | HIGH | Improper Access Control vulnerability in Apache Commons. A special BeanIntrospector class was added in version 1.9.2. This can be used to stop attackers from using the declared class property of Java enum objects to get access to the classloader. However this protection was not enabled by default. PropertyUtilsBean (and consequently BeanUtilsBean) now disallows declared class level property access by default. Releases 1.11.0 and 2.0.0-M2 address a potential security issue when accessing enum properties in an uncontrolled way. If an application using Commons BeanUtils passes property paths from an external source directly to the getProperty() method of PropertyUtilsBean, an attacker can access the enum’s class loader via the “declaredClass” property available on all Java “enum” objects. Accessing the enum’s “declaredClass” allows remote attackers to access the ClassLoader and execute arbitrary code. The same issue exists with PropertyUtilsBean.getNestedProperty(). Starting in versions 1.11.0 and 2.0.0-M2 a s | 1.11.0,1.11.0

When endpoint identification is enabled in the BCJSSE and an SSL socket is created without an explicit hostname (as happens with HttpsURLConnection), hostname verification could be performed against a DNS-resolved IP address in some situations, opening up a possibility of DNS poisoning. | 1.78

title: "Insufficient Site Isolation Against Spectre Vulnerability" reports incorrect alertRef for instances in json report. (pluginid: 90004)
description:
### Describe the bug: Insufficient Site Isolation Against Spectre Vulnerability reports incorrect alertRef for instances in json report. (pluginid: 90004) Consider this case to understand the issue: We scanned webgoat application and found that 15 instances of "Insufficient Site Isolation Against Spectre Vulnerability" were reported even though the systemic alerts limit was set to default (5). Reason: They have different alertRef. 15 instances: 5 instances for 90004-1 : Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy Header Missing or Invalid 5 instances for 90004-2 : Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy Header Missing or Invalid 5 instances for 90004-3 : Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy Header Missing or Invalid Attached screenshot for details. However, json report generated by ZAP reported all 15 instances with alertRef as 90004-1 as shown below (but in reality 5 instances have alertRef as 90004-2 and 5 instances have alertRef as 90004-3, there is no mentioned of 90004-2 or 90004-3 in json report): ``` { "pluginid": "90004", "alertRef": "90004-1", "alert": "Insufficient Site Isolation Against Spectre Vulnerability", "name": "Insufficient Site Isolation Against Spectre Vulnerability", "riskcode": "1", "confidence": "2", "riskdesc": "Low (Medium)", "desc": "

title: Graphql Addon URL schema no longer supports json type schema
description:
### Describe the bug:

Around Feb 14th (AEST) a weekly graphql scan that imported a JSON schema via URL started returning an error as below. (Note, using public examples for this report)

```
   Job graphql importing schema from URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/graphile/examples/67d34c4d22b72544fa134eb714610adb0ed73d3d/data/schema.json target: https://does1not1exist1example.net/graphql
   Unexpected error errors=[InvalidSyntaxError{ message=Invalid syntax with offending token '"data"' at line 2 column 3 ,offendingToken="data" ,locations=[SourceLocation{line=2, column=3}] ,sourcePreview={
  "data": {
    "__schema": {
      "queryType": {
        "name": "Query"
}] - see log for details
   Job graphql finished, time taken: 00:00:00
```

This error does not present if you same the schema.json locally and use local file import.

Automation to cause the error (noting warnings on load because no alerts configured):


[gql_schema_test.yaml](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25349702/gql_schema_test.yaml)


Saving the referenced schema file to the same directory and changing the above automation configuration to use `schemaFile: "schema.json"` successfully imports the file.

[https://raw.githubusercontent.com/graphile/examples/67d34c4d22b72544fa134eb714610adb0ed73d3d/data/schema.json](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/graphile/examples/67d34c4d22b72544fa134eb714610adb0ed73d3d/data/schema.json)

### Steps to reproduce the behavior:

Run the automation file attached to this issue.

[gql_schema_test.yaml](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25349702/gql_schema_test.yaml)

### Expected behavior:

The Graphql Add On be able to import the json based schema file by URL, which, depending on the service used for generation may start with a 'data' root element.

title: ZAP automation framework Openapi import not working in securecodebox
description:
### Describe the bug:

We have the securecodebox setup and zap automation framework scan type running in our AWS EKS cluster installed via ArgoCD & Helm. We are able to run a sample scan with basic authentication with the Scan Job CRDS and automation template configmaps. We have the DefectDojo UI and its hooks running on the same cluster. The scan reports are uploaded properly and we could see the vulnerability in DefectDojo UI. so basically end-to-end functions is working fine.

But the issue here is, we want to add API collections to the scan to check the security scan on these endpoints. We are exploring the openapi type job for fetching the endpoints from the file and add it to the scanner. But the logs were showing that it fetches number of URLs from the file but it seems like those endpoints were not added to the scan and its not their in the final report(DefectDojo).

We want the all the endpoints and its operations(request/post) should be scanned along with the other scans(Active/Spider).

I need some clarification on the followings,

* Is there a configuration missing to add these to the site tree, or is there any default restriction blocking?
* The logs show that openapi added 6 URLs and 5 URLs. Why are the added URLs not in the scan report?
* Share with me if there is any example document for using openapi jobs

### Steps to reproduce the behavior:

Please find the below scan configuration & configmaps and help me troubleshoot the issue.
```yaml
----
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: zap-automation-template
  namespace: securecodebox-system
data:
  automation-template.yaml: |
    env:
      parameters:
        logLevel: DEBUG
      contexts:
        - name: qportal-context
          urls: ["https://staging.example.com","https://api.staging.example.com"]
          includePaths:
            - "https://staging.example.com.*"
            - "https://api.staging.example.com.*"
            - "https://api.auth.staging.example.com.*"
            - "https://api.staging.example.com/api/.*"
          excludePaths:
            - ".*logout.*"
          authentication:
            method: json
            parameters:
              loginPageUrl: "https://staging.example.com/login"
              loginRequestUrl: "https://api.auth.staging.example.com/api/v3/users/login"
              loginRequestBody: '{"username":"aaaaaa@bbbb.com","password":"abssfsd@123","organisation_id":"d6102806-7edc-428c-b599-346324cb87ba"}'
            verification:
              method: response
              loggedInRegex: '(?i)(successful|token)'
              loggedOutRegex: '(?i)(error|invalid)'
          users:
            - name: qportal-user
              credentials:
                username: aaaaaa@bbbb.com
                password: abssfsd@123
          sessionManagement:
            method: http
    jobs:
      - type: passiveScan-wait
        parameters:
          maxDuration: 10
      # Spider + scan will be appended automatically after OpenAPI jobs
